annotations.label: Cayley–Hamilton theorem

anchor.snippet: satisfies its own characteristic equation

annotations.note: `Matrix.aeval_self_charpoly` states `aeval M M.charpoly = 0`, exactly the Cayley–Hamilton theorem.

annotations.note: `Matrix.charpoly` is defined as `det (charmatrix M)`, the characteristic polynomial of a square matrix.

annotations.note: `Matrix.pow_eq_aeval_mod_charpoly` writes `M^k = aeval M (X^k %ₘ charpoly)`, a polynomial of degree `< n`, covering `A^n` as a combination of lower powers.

anchors.value: {\displaystyle c_{n-m}={\frac {(-1)^{m}}{m!}}{\begin{vmatrix}\operatorname {tr} A&m-1&0&\cdots \\\operatorname {tr} A^{2}&\operatorname {tr} A&m-2&\cdots \\\vdots &\vdots &&&\vdots \\\operatorname {tr} A^{m-1}&\operatorname {tr} A^{m-2}&\cdots &\cdots &1\\\operatorname {tr} A^{m}&\operatorname {tr} A^{m-1}&\cdots &\cdots &\operatorname {tr} A\end{vmatrix}}~.}

annotations.label: Theorem holds for diagonalizable matrices

anchor.snippet: if A is diagonalizable , then the Cayley–Hamilton theorem must hold for A

annotations.note: Mathlib proves Cayley–Hamilton directly over any commutative ring, so the diagonalizable case is subsumed but not stated separately (no diagonalizable-matrix predicate).

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}p(A)\cdot v&=A^{n}\cdot v+c_{n-1}A^{n-1}\cdot v+\cdots +c_{1}A\cdot v+c_{0}I_{n}\cdot v\\[6pt]&=\lambda ^{n}v+c_{n-1}\lambda ^{n-1}v+\cdots +c_{1}\lambda v+c_{0}v=p(\lambda )v,\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Diagonalizable matrices are dense

anchor.snippet: diagonalizable complex square matrices of a given size is dense in the set of all such square matrices

annotations.note: Density of diagonalizable complex matrices is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean division by a monic polynomial

anchor.section: A synthesis of the first two proofs
anchor.snippet: Euclidean division by a monic polynomial P is defined, and always produces a unique quotient and remainder

annotations.label: adj(A) is a polynomial in A

anchor.snippet: adj( A ) can always be expressed as a polynomial in A

annotations.note: `Matrix.adjugate` is defined and used (as `adjugate (charmatrix M)`) inside the charpoly proof, but its expression as a polynomial in `A` is not a standalone lemma.

annotations.label: Permanent counterexample to bogus proof

anchor.section: A bogus "proof": p ( A ) = det( AI n − A ) = det( A − A ) = 0
anchor.snippet: if we consider the permanent function

annotations.note: `Matrix.permanent` is defined, but the pedagogical counterexample showing the bogus proof fails is not a formalized statement.

annotations.label: Theorem over any commutative ring

anchor.snippet: holds for matrices with entries in any commutative ring

annotations.note: `Matrix.aeval_self_charpoly` is already stated for matrices over an arbitrary commutative ring `R`.
